ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Aglio e Olio; closest parent 'pasta food product' (FOODON_00002473) used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Pasta, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific Aglio e Olio entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 'Pasta dish' is the closest parent concept; no specific Aglio e Olio code exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Aglio e Olio pasta is approximately 300 g, comprising a full plate of cooked spaghetti or penne tossed in garlic-infused olive oil with chili flakes.

nutrient_source.title: Pasta, cooked, enriched, without added salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 20121)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base pasta values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked enriched pasta (FDC ID 168936). Olive oil contribution (approximately 10 g per 100 g of dish) estimated from USDA FDC ID 171413 (Olive oil). Garlic and chili flakes contribute negligible macros at typical usage levels. Composite per-100g values calculated by weighted combination: ~75% cooked pasta + ~10% olive oil + ~15% water/aromatics. Sodium estimated conservatively as dish is prepared without added salt at base; restaurant preparation may add salt.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aglio e Olio as a composite dish. GI of 45 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked spaghetti (white, boiled 10–15 min, GI ~44–46, mean 45). Olive oil and garlic additions do not raise GI and may slightly lower it due to fat content slowing gastric emptying. GI category: low (below 55). Glycemic load per 300 g serving calculated as: (45 × 26.5 × 3.0) / 100 = 35.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content and restaurant preparation variability
hypertension.guidance: A standard 300 g restaurant serving of Aglio e Olio may contribute approximately 285 mg sodium from base preparation; individuals with hypertension should request reduced salt and be aware that restaurant versions may contain significantly more sodium than home-prepared versions.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined pasta carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 45 (low) but a glycemic load of ~35.6 per full restaurant serving, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion (150 g) to limit postprandial glucose excursion, and prefer al dente cooking which further lowers the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Predominantly monounsaturated fat from olive oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Aglio e Olio is prepared with extra-virgin olive oil, which provides monounsaturated fatty acids (approximately 4.8 g per 100 g) associated with cardioprotective effects; saturated fat is low at approximately 1.0 g per 100 g, making this dish relatively favourable for cardiovascular health when consumed in moderate portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aglio e Olio (Italian: 'garlic and oil') is a classic Southern Italian pasta dish originating from Naples, consisting of cooked spaghetti or penne tossed in a sauce of thinly sliced or minced garlic gently sautéed in high-quality extra-virgin olive oil, finished with dried chili flakes and fresh parsley. The dish is celebrated for its simplicity, requiring only a handful of pantry ingredients, yet delivering a deeply savoury, aromatic flavour profile with a mild heat from the chili. The texture is silky and lightly coated rather than sauced. In Singapore, Aglio e Olio is widely served in Italian restaurants, Western-casual dining, and Middle Eastern fusion establishments such as Sahara Bar & Restaurant. Nutritionally, the dish is moderate in energy (~185 kcal per 100 g), provides complex carbohydrates from pasta, and derives most of its fat from olive oil, which is rich in heart-healthy monounsaturated fatty acids. It is naturally dairy-free and vegan. The glycemic index is low (~45) due to the pasta matrix, though the glycemic load per full serving is moderate, making portion control relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is variable depending on preparation; restaurant versions may be higher. The dish is suitable for vegetarians and vegans and is halal-compliant in most preparations.

confidence_reason: No exact Aglio e Olio entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are derived from a defensible weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked pasta and olive oil. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked spaghetti, which is the primary ingredient. Confidence is medium rather than high because the composite estimation introduces uncertainty in fat and sodium values depending on restaurant preparation.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON was 'Middle Eastern' reflecting the restaurant (Sahara Bar & Restaurant); however, Aglio e Olio is an Italian dish and has been reclassified accordingly. The restaurant context is preserved in meal_context. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero: olive oil and plain pasta contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: this dish contains no animal-derived ingredients in its base formulation; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero: no animal products are present in the base dish; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this composite dish in the adapted USDA source; garlic and parsley contribute minor amounts but are not quantified in the composite model. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for cooked pasta or olive oil. 7. Multilingual names for Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi are provided to satisfy Singaporean dish requirements, as this dish is served in Singapore. Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese fields are null as this is not a dish of those cuisines. 8. Serving size of 300 g is an estimate for a standard restaurant plate; actual restaurant portions may vary from 250–400 g. 9. Sodium of 95 mg per 100 g is a conservative base estimate; restaurant preparations with added salt may reach 200–400 mg per 100 g.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected cuisine from 'Middle Eastern' (restaurant tag) to 'Italian' (dish origin).
Added multilingual names for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy Singapore-served dish requirements.
Constructed composite nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked pasta and olive oil.
Documented all -1 sentinel values with explicit reasons in notes.
Documented all 0.0 values for trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and vitamin_b12_mcg with 'confirmed zero' justification phrases in notes.
Set glycemic_index to 45 (numeric >0) from University of Sydney GI Database analogous estimate for cooked spaghetti.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases list are perfectly consistent.
Used only allowed ontology match_type values: 'exact', 'closest_parent', 'none'.
